metadata.title: Grade 7 Zambia ECZ Revision Exam Master Database

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Put your books ... that desk.
questions.explanation: 'On' is the correct preposition for placing items on a surface. 'Put your books on that desk' means placing them on top of the desk.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I bought a blue dress ... I like blue.
questions.explanation: 'Because' introduces a reason/cause. The reason she bought a blue dress is that she likes blue. 'Because' correctly joins the cause and effect.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: There are ... books in this box.
questions.explanation: 'Many' is the correct quantifier for countable nouns (books). 'Much' is for uncountable nouns. 'Plenty' and 'lots' require 'of' after them to be grammatically correct ('plenty of books', 'lots of books').

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Will you please come ... me to town?
questions.explanation: 'Come with me' is the correct expression for accompanying someone. 'With' indicates accompaniment/company.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I know you ... like swimming.
questions.explanation: The subject is 'you', which takes 'don't' (do not) for negation. 'Doesn't' is for third person singular (he/she/it). 'I know you don't like swimming' is grammatically correct.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The headteacher sat ... two pupils.
questions.explanation: 'Between' is used when referring to a position in the middle of two specific people or things. 'Between two pupils' is correct. 'Besides' means in addition to.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The thief ... stole the bicycle has been arrested.
questions.explanation: 'Who' is the relative pronoun used for people as the subject of a relative clause. 'The thief who stole the bicycle' — 'who' refers to the thief (a person) and acts as the subject of 'stole'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: All the girls came to check for the examination results, I don't know who told ...
questions.explanation: 'All the girls' is a plural subject, so the object pronoun must be 'them'. 'I don't know who told them' refers to the girls collectively.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: My mother ... nice scones.
questions.explanation: Scones are baked in an oven. The correct verb for making scones (and bread, cakes) is 'bakes'. You cook or boil other foods, but baked goods are 'baked'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: ... did not grow maize because they had no fertilizer.
questions.explanation: 'They' is the subject pronoun required at the start of a sentence as the subject of the verb 'did not grow'. 'Their' is possessive, 'Them' is an object pronoun, 'There' is an adverb/existential.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Yesterday we ... to the market to buy soap.
questions.explanation: 'Yesterday' signals past tense. 'Went' is the simple past tense of 'go'. 'Go' is present, 'goes' is third person present, 'going' requires an auxiliary verb.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The log was too ... for a small boy to lift.
questions.explanation: After 'too', the base form of the adjective is used: 'too heavy'. 'Too' + adjective + infinitive is the correct structure (too heavy to lift).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mulenga has the ... car in town.
questions.explanation: 'The' before the blank and 'in town' signals a superlative is needed. 'Best' is the superlative of 'good'. 'The best car in town' means no car is better.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: It is either Peter goes to the market ... he cleans the kitchen.
questions.explanation: 'Either ... or' is the correct correlative conjunction. 'Either Peter goes to the market or he cleans the kitchen' presents two alternatives.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A large crowd came to watch the game between Zambia and Congo ... of the bad weather.
questions.explanation: 'Despite' means in spite of, and is followed directly by a noun/noun phrase. 'Despite the bad weather' is correct. 'In spite' requires 'of' after it ('in spite of'), but is also acceptable — however 'despite' is more concise and fits the blank correctly here.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Having sold several bags of maize at K50,000 each, the farmer realized that his business still operated at a loss. The words at a loss mean ...
questions.explanation: 'At a loss' in business means operating without making a profit — spending more than is earned. 'Did not realise profit' is the correct meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Zambian Christians belong to different denominations. The word denominations means that ...
questions.explanation: 'Denominations' refers to different religious groups or sects within a religion. In the context of Zambian Christians, it refers to different religious/Christian groups (e.g., Catholic, Protestant, Seventh Day Adventist). 'Religious groups' is the broadest and most accurate answer.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: On the way to the river she met a stranger. The word stranger in the sentence means ...
questions.explanation: A 'stranger' is a person who is not known to you — an unknown person. 'Unknown' is the correct meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mrs Musonda gave no attention or care to the crying baby. The words no attention or care mean ...
questions.explanation: Giving 'no attention or care' to someone means you have neglected them. 'Neglected' is the word that means failing to give proper care or attention.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Most citizens are ignorant of their rights. The word ignorant means ...
questions.explanation: 'Ignorant' means lacking knowledge or awareness of something. 'Unaware' is the closest correct meaning — the citizens do not know about their rights.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: If she had come on time, she wouldn't have been late for lessons. This sentence means she ...
questions.explanation: The third conditional 'If she had come on time, she wouldn't have been late' implies she did NOT come on time, therefore she WAS late for lessons. Option A correctly states the actual outcome.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I spend all my time alone because I feel shy to talk to other people. This sentence means ...

options.A: I am free to be with other people.
options.B: I am not comfortable in the company of people.

questions.explanation: Feeling shy to talk to others and spending time alone means the person is not comfortable in the company of others. Option B best captures the meaning of shyness in social situations.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The baby cried immediately the mother arrived. This sentence means the ...

options.B: baby cried only when the mother arrived.
options.C: baby was crying before the mother arrived.
options.D: baby cried sometime after the mother arrived.

questions.explanation: 'Immediately the mother arrived' means the baby cried at the exact moment the mother arrived — not before, not after. Option B correctly states the baby cried only when (at the moment) the mother arrived.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The computer has done more good than bad to the society. This sentence means the computer is ...
questions.explanation: 'Done more good than bad' directly means the computer's benefits outweigh its harms — it is more useful than harmful.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Rice is one of the crops grown in Zambia. This sentence means rice ...

options.A: is the only crop grown in Zambia.
options.D: and other crops are grown in Zambia.

questions.explanation: 'One of the crops' means rice is among several crops — implying other crops are also grown. Option D correctly captures this meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A ... should be kept clean all the time.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'classroom'. It is a compound of 'class' + 'room', with double 's' from 'class' and double 'o' from 'room'. Options A, C, and D all have spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Donald is a ... young man. He will not leave the room dirty.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'responsible'. It ends in '-ible'. Options A, B, and C all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: My story has only one ...
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'paragraph'. It ends in '-graph'. Options B, C, and D all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Zambia is a ... because it is ruled by a president.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'republic'. It is spelled r-e-p-u-b-l-i-c. Options A, B, and D all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The wedding parcel was ... in a colourful paper.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'wrapped'. It doubles the 'p' before adding '-ed' (wrap → wrapped). Options A, C, and D all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: Put, mangoes, guavas and bananas in the same basket.
options.B: Put mangoes, guavas and bananas in the same basket.
options.C: Put mangoes guavas, and bananas, in the same basket.
options.D: Put mangoes guavas and bananas in the same basket.

questions.explanation: When listing items in a command, commas separate the items. 'Put mangoes, guavas and bananas' correctly uses commas between the items in the list without an unnecessary comma after 'Put'. Option B is correctly punctuated.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: As it started, raining people came running into the room.
options.B: As it started raining, people, came running into the room.
options.C: As it started raining people came running into the room.
options.D: As it started raining, people came running into the room.

questions.explanation: A comma is placed after an introductory adverbial clause ('As it started raining') before the main clause. Option D correctly places the comma after 'raining' before 'people came running'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: "I am very sorry my friend," said Mrs Mwape.
options.B: "I am very sorry, my friend," said Mrs Mwape.
options.C: I am very sorry, "my friend said Mrs Mwape."
options.D: "I am very sorry," my friend, said Mrs Mwape.

questions.explanation: When addressing someone directly ('my friend'), a comma must follow the address. Additionally, direct speech is enclosed in quotation marks. Option B correctly uses a comma after 'sorry' to set off the direct address 'my friend', with another comma before the closing quote.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: Our "School" Motto is Together we stand.
options.B: Our School" Motto is Together we stand."
options.C: "Our School Motto" is Together we stand.
options.D: Our School motto is, "Together we stand."

questions.explanation: A motto is a quoted phrase. The quotation marks should surround the motto itself ('Together we stand.'). Option D correctly places quotation marks around the motto and uses a comma to introduce it.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: "Yes, Sir, he said, theres nobody else here."
options.B: Yes, Sir, he said, "There's nobody else here."
options.C: "Yes, Sir" he said, "there's nobody else here."
options.D: "Yes, Sir," he said, "there's nobody else here."

questions.explanation: The direct speech has two parts: 'Yes, Sir,' and 'there's nobody else here.' Both are enclosed in separate quotation marks. A comma after 'Sir,' closes the first quote. 'he said,' introduces the second quote. Option D correctly punctuates this split direct speech.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the BEST order.

options.A: Mr Tembo is 60 years old. He has been driving for the last 40 years. He has never been involved in road accident. He always keeps his vehicle in good condition.
options.B: He always keeps his vehicle in good condition. He has been driving for the last 40 years. Mr Tembo is 60 years old. He has never been involved in road accident.
options.C: He has never been involved in road accident. He has been driving for the last 40 years. He always keeps his vehicle in good condition. Mr Tembo is 60 years old.
options.D: He has been driving for the last 40 years. He has never been involved in road accident. Mr Tembo is 60 years old. He always keeps his vehicle in good condition.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce Mr Tembo (age) → state his driving experience → note his safety record → explain why (keeps vehicle in good condition). Option A follows this natural biographical sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the BEST order.

options.A: Farming is good business. After selling the maize, she bought a grinding meal. Mrs Banda is a hard working farmer. She used some of the money to send her children to school. Last year she produced one thousand bags of maize.
options.B: Last year she produced one thousand bags of maize. She used some of the money to send her children to school. Farming is good business. Mrs Banda is a hard working farmer. After selling the maize, she bought a grinding meal.
options.C: After selling the maize, she bought a grinding meal. Last year she produced one thousand bags of maize. Mrs Banda is a hard working farmer. She used some of the money to send her children to school. Farming is good business.
options.D: Mrs Banda is a hard working farmer. Last year she produced one thousand bags of maize. After selling the maize, she bought a grinding meal. She used some of the money to send her children to school. Farming is good business.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce Mrs Banda as a hard working farmer → what she produced → what she did after selling → how she used the money → conclusion (farming is good business). Option D follows this narrative sequence correctly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the BEST order.

options.A: He saw a bad accident. Longwani was playing by the road side. When he went to sleep he had a nightmare. This has never come out of his mind.
options.B: This has never come out of his mind. Longwani was playing by the road side. When he went to sleep he had a nightmare. He saw a terrible accident.
options.C: Longwani was playing by the road side. He saw a terrible accident. When he went to sleep he had a nightmare. This has never come out of his mind.
options.D: He saw a terrible accident. This has never come out of his mind. When he went to sleep he had a nightmare. Longwani was playing by the road side.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: Longwani is playing by the roadside → he sees an accident → the accident haunts him in his sleep → it stays in his mind. Option C follows this chronological and cause-and-effect sequence correctly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the BEST order.

options.A: I got a phone call from Lusaka that I was needed to attend a workshop in Siavonga. I started off from Ndola at 10.00 hours. After travelling for 10 hours, I eventually arrived in Siavonga around 20.00 hours.
options.B: I started off from Ndola at 10.00 hours. After travelling for 10 hours, I eventually arrived in Siavonga around 20.00 hours. I got a phone call from Lusaka that I was needed to attend a workshop in Siavonga.
options.C: After travelling for 10 hours, I eventually arrived in Siavonga around 20.00 hours. I started off from Ndola at 10.00 hours. I got a phone call from Lusaka that I was needed to attend a workshop in Siavonga.
options.D: I got a phone call from Lusaka that I was needed to attend a workshop in Siavonga. After travelling for 10 hours, I eventually arrived in Siavonga around 20.00 hours. I started off from Ndola at 10.00 hours.

questions.explanation: The logical chronological order is: receive a phone call about the workshop → depart from Ndola → arrive in Siavonga after 10 hours. Option A follows this correct sequence of events.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the BEST order.

options.A: Dabwitso was very rich. They had everything they needed. He was so rich that he bought a big house where he lived with his family. His house and property were destroyed. One day an earthquake occurred.
options.B: One day an earthquake occurred. His house and property were destroyed. Dabwitso was very rich. He was so rich that he bought a big house where he lived with his family. They had everything they needed.
options.C: Dabwitso was very rich. He was so rich that he bought a big house where he lived with his family. They had everything they needed. One day an earthquake occurred. His house and property were destroyed.
options.D: Dabwitso was very rich. One day an earthquake occurred. His house and property were destroyed. They had everything they needed. He was so rich that he bought a big house where he lived with his family.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce Dabwitso's wealth → describe his house → describe their comfortable life → earthquake happens → destruction follows. Option C correctly presents this chronological narrative.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the BEST order.

options.A: He was imprisoned for 27 years. He then became the first black president of South Africa. He was finally released from prison in 1990. Mandela was a true freedom fighter.
options.B: Mandela was a true freedom fighter. He then became the first black president of South Africa. He was imprisoned for 27 years. He was finally released from prison in 1990.
options.C: Mandela was a true freedom fighter. He was imprisoned for 27 years. He was finally released from prison in 1990. He then became the first black president of South Africa.
options.D: He then became the first black president of South Africa. He was imprisoned for 27 years. Mandela was a true freedom fighter. He was finally released from prison in 1990.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce Mandela as a freedom fighter → he was imprisoned → released in 1990 → became first black president. Option C follows the correct chronological sequence of Mandela's life.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the BEST order.

options.A: Write a letter to the radio broadcaster. Thank him for the good work he is doing. In the letter, remember to say that his music selection is good. Tell him of the things you would want to hear more in future.
options.B: In the letter, remember to say that his music selection is good. Thank him for the good work he is doing. Write a letter to the radio broadcaster. Tell him of the things you would want to hear more in future.
options.C: Tell him of the things you would want to hear more in future. Write a letter to the radio broadcaster. In the letter, remember to say that his music selection is good. Thank him for the good work he is doing.
options.D: Thank him for the good work he is doing. In the letter, remember to say that his music selection is good. Tell him of the things you would want to hear more in future. Write a letter to the radio broadcaster.

questions.explanation: The logical order for letter-writing instructions is: decide to write the letter → thank him → praise his music → tell him what you want in future. Option A begins with the main action (write a letter) and follows with specific instructions in a logical sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Name the two main nutrients found in pineapple ...
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'The Pineapple is loaded with vitamins and minerals.' These are the two main nutrients specifically named in the passage.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A pineapple has ... elements that help keep the body healthy.
questions.explanation: The passage states pineapple has 'many health properties' and lists multiple nutrients (calcium, potassium, fibre, vitamin C, bromelain). 'Many' elements is the correct answer.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Calcium and potassium in pineapple are required for ...
questions.explanation: Calcium and potassium are minerals well known for supporting bone and teeth health. The passage mentions these as nutrients in pineapple. Based on nutritional knowledge, calcium and potassium are required for bones and teeth.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The word juice in the story means ...
questions.explanation: Juice is the liquid extracted from fruits. 'Liquid' is the most accurate meaning of 'juice' in this context.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: It is true to say that pineapples can be used as ...
questions.explanation: The passage states pineapple 'is high in the enzyme bromelain and the antioxidant vitamin C, both of which play a major role in the body's healing process.' The healing role supports the use of pineapple as medicine.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The best title for this story can be ...
questions.explanation: The passage discusses the health benefits, nutritional value, and uses of pineapple — all aspects of its importance. 'Importance of pineapple' is the most comprehensive title that covers all the passage's content.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Slaves were used to ...
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'The slaves were expected to work extra hard on the plantations.' Slaves were used to work on the plantations in America.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The word era in the story means ...
questions.explanation: 'Era' means a long and distinct period of history. 'Period of time' is the most accurate and general meaning of 'era'. A decade, century, or millennium are all specific lengths, while 'era' is more general.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: In the Triangular Slave Trade, America supplied ...
questions.explanation: The Triangular Slave Trade involved three continents: Europe supplied manufactured goods, Africa supplied slaves and raw materials, and the Americas supplied agricultural products from the plantations. Based on the passage structure and historical context, America supplied manufactured goods in return.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The Triangular Slave Trade involved ... continents.
questions.explanation: The trade is called 'Triangular' because it formed a triangle between three continents: Europe, Africa, and America. 'Triangular' itself implies three points/continents.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: From the story, which continent did not benefit from the trade?
questions.explanation: Africa was exploited as the source of cheap labour (slaves) and raw materials. Africa did not benefit from the trade — it was the victim. Europe and America benefited economically. Asia is not mentioned in the passage.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: In the story "quantity and quality were not considered" means that trade was ...
questions.explanation: When quantity and quality are not considered, trade is done carelessly and without proper standards — which is unfair. The passage says this was the primitive manner of trade. 'Unfairly done' is the correct interpretation.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The word unique in the story means ...
questions.explanation: 'Unique' means being the only one of its kind — different from all others. The passage says Scouting is 'unique' meaning it is different from other youth organisations.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Leaders guide young people because ...

options.B: young people don't go in camp.

questions.explanation: The passage says 'It is the responsibility of Scout leaders to guide the young people, so leaders should know what it is all about.' Leaders guide because they are knowledgeable and experienced about Scouting.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The Scout Movement is ...

options.D: found in the Promise and Law.

questions.explanation: The passage states 'Scouting is a Movement for young people' and compares it to 'many other youth organisations'. The Scout Movement is described as a youth organisation.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Young people are attracted to the Scout Movement by ...
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Most of the young people who enter the Scout Movement are attracted by the outdoor life, the camping' and 'all those activities which are attractive to boys and girls'. Activities and camping attract young people.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: "What makes it so is something ..." The word 'so' in the sentence refers to the word ...
questions.explanation: The sentence 'Scouting is however, unique. What makes it so is something called "ethos"' — the word 'so' refers back to 'unique'. 'So' is a pronoun substitute for the adjective 'unique'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What is the best title of this story?
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on what makes Scouting unique — the 'ethos' or spirit. The essence of Scouting is the spirit found in the Scout Promise and the Law. 'The Spirit of Scouting' is the most accurate and comprehensive title for the passage.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: We like stories. Please tell ... one.
questions.explanation: The subject is 'We', so the object pronoun must be 'us'. 'Please tell us one' means tell one to us (the group). 'Me' is singular, 'you' and 'them' refer to other parties.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: She came in ... her mother was eating.
questions.explanation: 'While' indicates two actions happening at the same time. 'She came in while her mother was eating' shows simultaneous events. 'Before' would mean she came in before the eating started.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Chewe came late ... she managed to find a seat in the school hall.
questions.explanation: 'But' is used to show contrast. Despite coming late, she still found a seat — these are contrasting ideas. 'But' correctly joins the two contrasting clauses.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I enjoy ... fish.
questions.explanation: The verb 'enjoy' is followed by a gerund (verb+ing). 'I enjoy eating fish' is the grammatically correct form. 'Enjoy' cannot be followed by an infinitive ('to eat').

questions.subject: English
questions.question: She likes to jog ... night.
questions.explanation: The correct preposition for specific times of day (night, noon, midnight) is 'at'. 'She likes to jog at night' is the standard English expression.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mutinta is ... the kitchen walls.
questions.explanation: 'Is ... painting' forms the present continuous tense (is + verb-ing), indicating an action happening right now. The sentence describes Mutinta in the act of painting the walls.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Did you see the boy ... got your pen?
questions.explanation: 'Who' is the relative pronoun used for people as the subject of a relative clause. 'The boy who got your pen' — 'who' refers to the boy (a person) and acts as the subject of 'got'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: George lived with ... uncle in Chingola for two years.
questions.explanation: George is male, so the possessive pronoun must be 'his'. 'His uncle' indicates the uncle belongs to George. Object pronouns (him, her, them) cannot modify a noun.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: My cat is ... on the sofa.
questions.explanation: 'Is ... sleeping' forms the present continuous tense (is + verb-ing). The cat is currently in the act of sleeping on the sofa.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The learner was suspended ... stealing school books.
questions.explanation: The preposition 'for' is used to indicate the reason or cause of an action. 'Suspended for stealing' means the reason for suspension was stealing.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The sheep was attacked by eight ...
questions.explanation: The correct plural of 'wolf' is 'wolves'. Words ending in 'f' or 'fe' typically form their plural by changing 'f' to 'v' and adding 'es'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: ... Chansa nor Kamedula came to school on time.
questions.explanation: 'Neither ... nor' is the correct correlative conjunction for negative statements about two subjects. 'Neither Chansa nor Kamedula came' means not Chansa and not Kamedula.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: China is one of the ... developed nations in the world.
questions.explanation: 'One of the most' is the correct superlative construction. When using 'one of the', the superlative form 'most' is required followed by a plural noun.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Of all the bridges I have seen, Rusinda is the ...
questions.explanation: 'Of all the bridges' signals superlative degree. 'Narrowest' is the correct one-word superlative form of 'narrow'. 'Most narrow' is also grammatically possible but 'narrowest' is the preferred form.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Yesterday the market had a ... of people.
questions.explanation: The correct collective noun for a large group of people gathered together is 'crowd'. 'Bunch' is for flowers/fruit, 'herd' is for cattle, 'pack' is for wolves or cards.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: We must look ... into the cause of the fire.
questions.explanation: 'Deeply' is an adverb that modifies the verb 'look'. Adverbs (not adjectives) modify verbs. 'Look deeply into' means to investigate thoroughly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: All the children in the village were vaccinated ... polio.
questions.explanation: The correct collocation is 'vaccinated against' a disease. 'Vaccinated against polio' is the standard medical English expression.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mr Hamaundu is a commercial farmer and has a ... of cattle at his farm.
questions.explanation: The correct collective noun for cattle is 'herd'. 'A herd of cattle' is the standard English expression.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: She sang so ... that the minister gave her a reward.
questions.explanation: 'Well' is the adverb form of 'good' and is used to modify the verb 'sang'. 'She sang so well' is correct. 'Good' is an adjective and cannot modify a verb.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Dora has become ... than she was in her previous job.
questions.explanation: A comparison between two states (current job vs. previous job) requires the comparative form. 'Busier than' is the correct comparative of 'busy' (short adjective: y → ier).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: We go to church on ...
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'Saturdays'. The correct spelling is S-a-t-u-r-d-a-y-s. All other options rearrange the letters incorrectly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A ... never loses hope in spite of problems.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'believer'. It comes from the verb 'believe' + '-r'. Options A, B, and C all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A mother must not ... one of her children more than others.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'favour' (British English spelling). Options B, C, and D all contain incorrect letter arrangements.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Group the objects ... to their size and shape.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'according'. It has a double 'c': ac-cord-ing. Options B, C, and D all have spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: After his ... from prison, he came home.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'release'. It means to set free. 'Realise' means to become aware of something — a different word entirely. Options A and B are misspellings.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: Mr Chandas' wives are both teachers.
options.B: Mr Chanda's wives are both teachers.
options.C: Mr Chandas wives are both teachers.
options.D: Mr Chanda wives are both teachers.

questions.explanation: The name is 'Mr Chanda' (singular). The possessive of a singular name ending in 'a' is formed by adding 's: Chanda's. Option B correctly shows 'Mr Chanda's wives'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: "Have you seen my purse?" Mutinta's mother asked.
options.B: "have you seen my purse?" mutinta's mother asked.
options.C: "Have you seen my purse?" mutinta's mother, asked.
options.D: "have you seen my purse?" Mutinta's mother asked.

questions.explanation: Direct speech begins with a capital letter inside the quotation marks. The reporting clause 'Mutinta's mother asked' begins with a capital M as it is a proper noun. Option A is correctly punctuated throughout.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.C: Be quiet! shouted the class monitor.

questions.explanation: Exclamatory direct speech is enclosed in quotation marks with the exclamation mark inside the closing quote. The reporting clause is outside the quotes. Option B is correctly punctuated.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: You can either use a Cellphone, fax, a letter, or land phone to send a message.
options.B: You can either use a cellphone, fax, a letter or land phone to send a message.
options.C: You can either use a cellphone, fax, a letter or land phone to send a message?
options.D: "You can either use a cellphone, fax, a letter or land phone to send a message."

questions.explanation: This is a statement (not a question or direct speech), so it ends with a full stop. The items in the list are separated by commas. Option B has correct comma usage between list items and ends with a full stop. Option A capitalises 'Cellphone' incorrectly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: He told his friends where he was going.
options.B: He told his friends "where he was going."
options.C: "He told his friends where he was going?"
options.D: "He told his friends, "where he was going.

questions.explanation: This is indirect/reported speech, not direct speech, so no quotation marks are needed. The sentence is a statement and ends with a full stop. Option A is the only correctly punctuated version.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The teacher reminded us not to erase the notes on the board. The word erase means ...
questions.explanation: 'Erase' means to rub out or remove writing. In the context of a board, erasing means rubbing off the notes. 'Rub' is the closest meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: When Margaret saw her sister give her whole salary to the church, she said "charity begins at home." The underlined words mean Margaret's sister must ...

options.A: always give to the church and not to her family.
options.D: provide for to her family first before giving the church.

questions.explanation: The idiom 'charity begins at home' means one should take care of one's own family before helping others. Margaret is saying her sister should look after the family first before donating to the church.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: If Mumba had gone to China, he would have learnt Chinese. This sentence means Mumba ...

options.A: will go to China and will not learn Chinese.
options.B: went to China to learn Chinese.
options.D: did not go to China and did not learn Chinese.

questions.explanation: 'If Mumba had gone... he would have learnt' is a third conditional, expressing something that did not happen in the past. He did not go to China, therefore he did not learn Chinese.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: We have so many organisations that take care of widows and orphans in Zambia. The word widows means a woman whose ... has died.
questions.explanation: A 'widow' is a woman whose husband has died. This is the standard definition of the word.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mr Sikala put a poster written "First come, first served," at the entrance of his butchery. The underlined words mean ...

options.A: if one arrives first, one gets a better choice.
options.B: people are only served when they come first.
options.C: people get served first before they come.
options.D: you provide service to others when you are first.

questions.explanation: 'First come, first served' means that people will be attended to in the order they arrive — those who come first will be served first. Option B best captures this meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The apostle Paul was not ashamed of preaching the word of God. The word ashamed means that Paul was not ...
questions.explanation: 'Ashamed' means feeling embarrassed or guilty about something. Paul was not ashamed means he was not embarrassed to preach. 'Embarrassed' is the closest correct meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: He gazed at the food for a long time. The word gazed means ...
questions.explanation: 'Gazed' means looked steadily and intently at something for a long time. 'Looked' is the closest and most accurate meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Either Musonda or Chileshe will attend their uncle's wedding this weekend. This sentence means ... of them will attend the wedding.
questions.explanation: 'Either ... or' means one or the other but not both. So only one of them will attend the wedding.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: You have to find suitable land. Cultivate it and make beds. Then plant the seeds and start watering them. Do you want to prepare a vegetable nursery?
options.B: Cultivate it and make beds. You have to find suitable land. Do you want to prepare a vegetable nursery? Then plant the seeds and start watering them.
options.C: Cultivate it and make beds. Do you want to prepare a vegetable nursery? You have to find suitable land. Then plant the seeds and start watering them.
options.D: Do you want to prepare a vegetable nursery? You have to find suitable land. Cultivate it and make beds. Then plant the seeds and start watering them.

questions.explanation: The logical order for instructions is: introduce the purpose (Do you want to prepare a vegetable nursery?) → find land → cultivate and make beds → plant seeds and water. Option D follows this logical sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Excuse me Madam. My name is Charity from Dyna Farms. I am selling tomatoes. I was wondering if you could take a look at our farm produce.
options.B: I was wondering if you could take a look at our farm produce. Excuse me Madam. I am selling tomatoes. My name is Charity from Dyna Farms.
options.C: Excuse me Madam. I was wondering if you could take a look at our farm produce. My name is Charity from Dyna Farms. I am selling tomatoes.
options.D: I am selling tomatoes. Excuse me Madam. My name is Charity from Dyna Farms. I was wondering if you could take a look at our farm produce.

questions.explanation: The logical conversational order is: get attention (Excuse me Madam) → introduce yourself (My name is Charity from Dyna Farms) → state purpose (I am selling tomatoes) → make a request (I was wondering if you could take a look). Option A follows natural conversational sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Zambia is a large country in the central part of Southern Africa. It has an area of 752,617 square kilometres. It is surrounded by other countries and has no sea coast. It is therefore a landlocked country. Lusaka is the capital city of Zambia.
options.B: Lusaka is the capital city of Zambia. It has an area of 752,617 square kilometres. It is surrounded by other countries and has no sea coast. It is therefore a landlocked country. Zambia is a large country in the central part of Southern Africa.
options.C: It is surrounded by other countries and has no sea coast. Zambia is a large country in the central part of Southern Africa. It has an area of 752,617 square kilometres. It is therefore a landlocked country. Lusaka is the capital city of Zambia.
options.D: Zambia is a large country in the central part of Southern Africa. It is therefore a landlocked country. It has an area of 752,617 square kilometres. It is surrounded by other countries and has no sea coast. Lusaka is the capital city of Zambia.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce Zambia → give its size → explain it is surrounded by countries → conclude it is landlocked → state the capital. Option A presents these facts in the most logical and coherent sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: A library is a place where you can find books, magazines, records and much more. It is a treasure house of information and entertainment. That is why you are encouraged to visit a library regularly. By so doing, you will not only gain knowledge but be entertained as well.
options.B: It is a treasure house of information and entertainment. A library is a place where you can find books, magazines, records and much more. That is why you are encouraged to visit a library regularly. By so doing, you will not only gain knowledge but be entertained as well.
options.C: A library is a place where you can find books, magazines, records and much more. That is why you are encouraged to visit a library regularly. It is a treasure house of information and entertainment. By so doing, you will not only gain knowledge but be entertained as well.
options.D: It is a treasure house of information and entertainment. That is why you are encouraged to visit a library regularly. A library is a place where you can find books, magazines, records and much more. By so doing, you will not only gain knowledge but be entertained as well.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: define a library → describe what it is (treasure house) → reason to visit → benefit of visiting. Option A presents the most logical and coherent paragraph structure.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Mrs Mubisi was my teacher of English at Naboye Secondary School. She was my favourite teacher. I liked her sense of humour but most learners feared her. She was very tough and firm.
options.B: I liked her sense of humour but most learners feared her. She was very tough and firm. Mrs Mubisi was my teacher of English at Naboye Secondary School. She was my favourite teacher.
options.C: Mrs Mubisi was my teacher of English at Naboye Secondary School. I liked her sense of humour but most pupils feared her. She was very tough and firm. She was my favourite teacher.
options.D: She was very tough and firm but most pupils feared her. She was my favourite teacher. Mrs Mubisi was my teacher of English at Naboye Secondary School. I liked her sense of humour.

questions.explanation: The logical order introduces Mrs Mubisi → states she is a favourite → gives reasons why (sense of humour) → contrasts with why others feared her (tough and firm). Option A flows naturally and coherently.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: They therefore lose very little water. Indeed, camels have strange bodies that do not sweat. The most amazing fact about camels is that they can go for a very long time without drinking water.
options.B: The most amazing fact about camels is that they can go for a very long time without drinking water. Indeed, camels have strange bodies that do not sweat. They therefore lose very little water.
options.C: Indeed, camels have strange bodies that do not sweat. They therefore lose very little water. The most amazing fact about camels is that they can go for a very long time without drinking water.
options.D: They therefore lose very little water. The most amazing fact about camels is that they can go for a very long time without drinking water. Indeed, camels have strange bodies that do not sweat.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: state the most amazing fact (go long without water) → explain why (strange bodies that don't sweat) → give the consequence (lose very little water). Option B follows this cause-and-effect reasoning correctly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Leadership is not easy. One has to be passionate, understanding and of good character. People like such individuals to be their leaders.
options.B: Leadership is not easy. People like such individuals to be their leaders. One has to be passionate, understanding and of good character.
options.C: One has to be passionate, understanding and of good character. Leadership is not easy. People like such individuals to be their leaders.
options.D: One has to be passionate, understanding and of good character. People like such individuals to be their leaders. Leadership is not easy.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce the topic (leadership is not easy) → state what is required (passion, understanding, good character) → conclude with public preference. Option A presents the most logical and coherent sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Who sleeps in the bedroom directly opposite the sitting room? The ...
questions.explanation: From the house plan, the Sitting Room is in the top-left. Directly opposite it (bottom-left) is the Visitors' Bedroom. Therefore, visitors sleep in the bedroom directly opposite the sitting room.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: How many bedrooms does Mr Manchishi's house have?
questions.explanation: From the house plan, there are four bedrooms: Master Bedroom, Visitors' Bedroom, Boys' Bedroom, and Girls' Bedroom. The total is four bedrooms.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The part of the house marked X is commonly known as the ...
questions.explanation: The area marked X on the house plan is the connecting corridor/hallway between rooms. This interior connecting area is commonly known as a passage.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mr Manchishi's family always complained of a bad smell from the toilet. What do you think was the problem? The toilet ...
questions.explanation: From the house plan, the Toilet/Shower has a door but no window symbol is shown for it. A toilet with no window would have poor ventilation, causing a bad smell to build up.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The bedroom which has two windows is next to the ...
questions.explanation: From the house plan, the Sitting Room has two windows on its left outer wall. The room next to it sharing the top row is the Kitchen. However, reviewing the plan again, the Master Bedroom has two windows at the top. The Master Bedroom is next to the Kitchen. The Sitting Room is next to the Sitting Room side. Based on the house plan, the room with two windows on its outer wall that is clearly next to the Sitting Room is the Sitting Room itself — but looking at the layout, the Master Bedroom has two windows and is adjacent to the Kitchen. The correct answer based on the house plan layout where the Master Bedroom (two windows on top wall) is next to the Kitchen is A: kitchen.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Why didn't the boys pick up the oranges that dropped?

options.C: They had enough in the bag.
options.D: They were scared of the cemetery.

questions.explanation: The passage states: 'they did not bother to pick them up since they had enough in the bag.' The reason they left the dropped oranges was that they already had enough.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Why did the drunkard run to a nearby church? To ...

options.B: inform the priest about his mystery.
options.D: steal some oranges from the orchard.

questions.explanation: The passage states the drunkard 'ran as fast as he could to a nearby church to report his mystery to the priest.' He went to inform the priest about what he heard (the mysterious voices).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The priest and the drunkard ran away from the cemetery because they ...

options.B: still wanted to live good lives.
options.C: thought the ghosts would share them.
options.D: wanted to tell others what they heard.

questions.explanation: When the voice said 'What about the two at the gate?' both men ran away shouting 'We are not dead yet! Please spare us!' They thought the ghosts were going to share/divide them as corpses.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What nutrients do we get from the fruit mentioned in the passage.
questions.explanation: The fruit mentioned in the passage is oranges. Oranges are well known for providing Vitamins, particularly Vitamin C. This is a general knowledge question linked to the passage context.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The word mystery in the passage means ...
questions.explanation: In context, the drunkard went to 'report his mystery to the priest' — he was reporting something strange and unexplained that he had witnessed. 'Mystery' here means something strange or puzzling.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What food does the larva eat?
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'When the larva emerges, it starts its life by eating the leaf it was born onto.' The larva eats leaves.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The word emerge in the passage means ...
questions.explanation: 'Emerge' means to come out or appear from somewhere. In the passage, 'a larva emerges' and 'the adult butterfly emerges' both describe coming out of something (egg/pupa).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What is the first stage in the life cycle of a butterfly?
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'The cycle begins when an adult butterfly lays a handful of tiny round eggs onto a leaf.' The first stage is the Egg stage.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A butterfly is an insect because it ...
questions.explanation: Insects are defined by having six legs (three pairs). While butterflies also have wings, having six legs is the defining characteristic that classifies them as insects.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage, the stubby caterpillar is the ... stage of a butterfly.
questions.explanation: The life cycle stages are: 1. Egg, 2. Larva (caterpillar), 3. Pupa, 4. Adult butterfly. The passage describes 'a short, stubby caterpillar' which is the larva — the second stage.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I have given this book to you. Therefore, it is ...
questions.explanation: The speaker gave the book to 'you', so the book now belongs to 'you'. The correct possessive pronoun is 'yours'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mwila remained in water ... a long time.
questions.explanation: The preposition 'for' is used to indicate a duration of time. 'Remained for a long time' is grammatically correct.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: All the pupils were given books ... Sepiso.
questions.explanation: 'Except' means excluding. 'All pupils except Sepiso' means everyone got books but Sepiso did not. This is the correct preposition of exclusion.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: He checked his punctured tube by ... it into a basin of water.
questions.explanation: After the preposition 'by', a gerund (verb+ing) must be used. 'By putting' is the correct form.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: John and Mary love ... parents.
questions.explanation: 'John and Mary' is a plural subject (they), so the possessive pronoun must be 'their'. 'Their parents' refers to the parents of both John and Mary.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: ... people study Spanish nowadays.
questions.explanation: 'Few' is used with countable nouns (people). 'A little' and 'Little' are used with uncountable nouns. 'Much' is also for uncountable nouns and does not fit this context.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: He is jealous ... his friend.
questions.explanation: The adjective 'jealous' collocates with the preposition 'of'. The correct phrase is 'jealous of'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The dog ... was shot died instantly.
questions.explanation: 'Which' is the correct relative pronoun for animals. 'Who' and 'whom' are for people, 'whose' indicates possession. 'The dog which was shot' is correct.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mulenga is ... cruel than Mwaka.
questions.explanation: A comparison between two people uses the comparative form 'more'. 'More cruel than' is a comparative construction.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mulima will go to the hospital ... you allow him.
questions.explanation: 'If' introduces a conditional clause. 'Mulima will go to the hospital if you allow him' expresses a condition. 'Unless' would mean 'if not', which reverses the meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I saw you pick up my pencil case ... you cannot deny it.
questions.explanation: 'And' joins two related statements: 'I saw you pick it up' and 'you cannot deny it'. Both statements are facts that logically follow each other.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: They were talking ... the others were eating.
questions.explanation: 'While' is used to show two actions happening at the same time. 'They were talking while the others were eating' shows simultaneous actions.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Chipo picked a ... of flowers for her grandmother.
questions.explanation: The correct collective noun for flowers is 'bunch'. We say 'a bunch of flowers', not bundle, group, or heap.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: How ... does the dispensary open?
questions.explanation: 'How often' is the correct interrogative phrase to ask about frequency. 'How always', 'how sometimes', and 'how usually' are not grammatically correct question forms.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: AIDS cases ... to be common in the 1980s.
questions.explanation: The sentence refers to a past event (1980s), so simple past tense is required. 'Started' is the correct past tense form for the plural subject 'AIDS cases'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: ... of our learners arrived at school late because it was raining in the morning.
questions.explanation: 'Several' means more than two but not very many, and is used with countable nouns (learners). 'Much' is for uncountable nouns. 'Plenty' requires 'of' to be grammatically natural here. 'Several' fits best.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Maria saw a ... of bees near the well.
questions.explanation: The correct collective noun for bees is 'swarm'. A swarm of bees is the standard English expression.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: When I reached home, the dog had already ... the food.
questions.explanation: 'Had already' requires the past participle. The past participle of 'eat' is 'eaten'. 'Had already eaten' is the correct past perfect form.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The noise that came from the stadium was the ...
questions.explanation: The superlative form 'loudest' is required because 'the' before the blank signals that this noise is being compared to all others. 'The loudest' is the correct superlative.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I saw a ... of lions in the Luangwa National Game Park yesterday.
questions.explanation: The correct collective noun for lions is 'pride'. A pride of lions is the standard English expression. 'Shoal' is for fish, 'swarm' is for bees/insects.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mathematics requires a lot of ...
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'calculations'. It comes from the root 'calculate'. Options B, C, and D all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mrs Banda is a ... because she gives advice to people with problems.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'counsellor' (one who counsels/gives advice). Options A, B, and C all have spelling errors in either the 'coun-' prefix or the '-llor' suffix.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The new pupils expressed ... about the school rules.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'ignorance'. It ends in '-ance', not '-anse'. Options A and C are identical misspellings; B has a double 'r' which is incorrect.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: During ... nurses are called upon any time.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'emergencies'. Both A and D appear the same; A is accepted as the first correctly spelled option. 'Emergensies' and 'imergencies' are misspellings.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A ... is a room where pupils in boarding schools sleep.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'dormitory'. It has one 'r' and one 'm'. Options A and D double the wrong letters; B omits the 'r'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: I would like you to stay in a more comfortable place Anna said to Sara.
options.B: I would like you to stay in a more comfortable place. Anna said to Sara,
options.C: I would like you to stay in a more comfortable place, "Anna said to Sara."
options.D: "I would like you to stay in a more comfortable place," Anna said to Sara.

questions.explanation: Direct speech must be enclosed in quotation marks, with a comma before the closing quote and the reporting clause outside. Option D correctly uses opening and closing quotation marks with a comma before the closing quote.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: You dont' like milk do you
options.B: You don't like milk? do you.
options.C: You don't like milk, do you?
options.D: You don't like milk, do you.

questions.explanation: A tag question requires a comma before the tag ('do you') and a question mark at the end. The contraction 'don't' also needs an apostrophe in the correct place. Option C is correctly punctuated.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe in possessive singular goes before the 's': John's. The sentence ends with a full stop. Option A correctly shows singular possessive and ends with a period.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: Sililo likes eating cassava, sweet potatoes, and apples.
options.B: Sililo likes eating cassava, sweet potatoes and apples
options.C: Sililo likes eating cassava? Sweet potatoes and apples,
options.D: Sililo likes eating cassava, sweet potatoes and apples.

questions.explanation: When listing three or more items, commas separate each item. Option A uses the Oxford comma (before 'and') and ends with a full stop, making it correctly punctuated.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: "They have scored!" exclaimed a soccer fan.
options.B: They have scored! exclaimed a soccer fan.
options.C: They have scored exclaimed a soccer fan!
options.D: "They have scored" exclaimed a soccer fan.

questions.explanation: Exclamatory direct speech requires quotation marks around the spoken words and an exclamation mark inside the closing quotation mark. Option A correctly places the exclamation mark inside the quotes and the reporting clause outside.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The Zambian Government has decided to carry out an awareness campaign. Awareness campaign means ...
questions.explanation: An 'awareness campaign' is an effort to inform or educate the public about something. 'Inform the public' is the correct meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: If Esther Phiri wins the heavy weight boxing title this weekend, she will have a party. The sentence tells us that Esther Phiri ...
questions.explanation: The sentence uses 'if', making the party conditional on winning. She has not yet won, so the party is only possible/might happen. 'Might have a party' correctly captures the conditional meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mr Kachasu failed to care for his family. This means that he ... his family.
questions.explanation: 'Failed to care for' means he did not look after them, which is the meaning of 'neglected'. To neglect is to fail to give proper care or attention.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The tourist used her binoculars to view the Victoria Falls. The word view means ...
questions.explanation: In this context, 'view' means to look at or see something. Binoculars are used to see things from a distance, so 'see' is the correct meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: He sustained multiple injuries. The word multiple means ...
questions.explanation: 'Multiple' means many or more than one. 'Multiple injuries' means he suffered many injuries.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The school headteacher used to play netball. This sentence means that she ...

options.A: does not play netball any more.
options.C: she is good at playing netball.

questions.explanation: 'Used to' indicates a past habit that no longer continues. The headteacher played netball in the past but no longer does so.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Thieves harass the public. The word harass means ...
questions.explanation: 'Harass' means to trouble, disturb, or intimidate someone repeatedly. 'Trouble' is the closest correct meaning among the options.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: He was forced to move on his hand and knees. This sentence means he was forced to ...
questions.explanation: Moving on hands and knees is the definition of crawling. 'Crawl' is the correct interpretation of the underlined phrase.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Mary didn't want to go to school. Her mother asked her why? She said that she preferred to play football. Then the mother gave her a good beating.
options.B: Her mother asked her why? She said that she preferred to play football. Mary didn't want to go to school. Then the mother gave her a good beating.
options.C: Then the mother gave her a good beating. Mary didn't want to school. She said that she preferred to play football. Her mother asked her why?
options.D: She said that she preferred to play football. Then the mother gave her a good beating. Her mother asked her why? Mary didn't want to go to school.

questions.explanation: The logical sequence of events is: Mary refuses school → mother asks why → Mary explains she prefers football → mother punishes her. Option A follows this natural cause-and-effect order.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: One day, Luyando had gone for prep in the evening. She studied up to 22:00 hours. When she went back to her room, she found her beddings had been stolen.
options.B: When she went back to her room she found her beddings had been stolen. One day, Luyando had gone for prep in the evening. She studied up to 22:00 hours.
options.C: One day, Luyando had gone for prep in the evening. When she went back to her room, she found her beddings had been stolen. She studied up to 22:00 hours.
options.D: When she went back to her room, she found her beddings had been stolen. She studied up to 22:00 hours. One day, Luyando had gone for prep in the evening.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: Luyando goes for prep → she studies until 22:00 → she returns to find beddings stolen. Option A follows the correct chronological sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Your teacher will read a passage to you. Listen carefully as you write it down. Be sure to use the correct spellings and punctuation marks.
options.B: Listen carefully as you write it down. Your teacher will read a passage to you. Be sure to use the correct spellings and punctuation marks.
options.C: Be sure to use the correct spellings and punctuation marks. Listen carefully as you write it down. Your teacher will read a passage to you.
options.D: Listen carefully as you write it down. Be sure to use the correct spellings and punctuation marks. Your teacher will read a passage to you.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: teacher reads a passage → student listens and writes → student ensures correct spelling and punctuation. Option A follows the natural instructional sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Suddenly, he heard a loud noise coming from the house. One Saturday morning, Mambwe was playing with his wire car behind their big and beautiful house. He stopped what he was doing and rushed towards the house.
options.B: One Saturday morning, Mambwe was playing with his wire car behind their big and beautiful house. He stopped what he was doing and rushed towards the house. Suddenly, he heard a loud noise coming from the house.
options.C: One Saturday morning, Mambwe was playing with his wire car behind their big and beautiful house. Suddenly, he heard a loud noise coming from the house. He stopped what he was doing and rushed to the house.
options.D: He stopped what he was doing and rushed to the house. One Saturday morning, Mambwe was playing with his wire car behind their big and beautiful house. Suddenly, he heard a loud noise coming from the house.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: Mambwe is playing → he hears a noise → he stops and rushes to the house. Option C places the setting first, then the trigger event, then the response.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: The new pupils were now happy. When the boys arrived at the school, they did not know what to do or where to go. Fortunately, a prefect greeted them and took them to the school hall where they were addressed by the headteacher.
options.B: Fortunately, a prefect greeted them and took them to the school hall, where they were addressed by the headteacher. When the boys arrived at the school, they did not know what to do or where to go. The new pupils were now happy.
options.C: When the boys arrived at the school, they did not know what to do or where to go. Fortunately, a prefect greeted them and took them to the school hall where they were addressed by the headteacher. The new pupils were now happy.
options.D: When the boys arrived at the school, they did not know what to do or where to go. The new pupils were now happy. Fortunately, a prefect greeted them and took them to the school hall where they were addressed by the headteacher.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: boys arrive and are confused → prefect helps them → they become happy. Option C follows this cause-and-effect sequence correctly, ending with the resolution.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Mr. Daka was proud of his sons. They were always good boys. When they grew up they did not forget about their parents.
options.B: Mr. Daka was proud of his sons. When they grew up they did not forget about their parents. They were always good boys.
options.C: They were always good boys. Mr. Daka was proud of his sons. When they grew up they did not forget about their parents.
options.D: When they grew up they did not forget about their parents. Mr. Daka was proud of his sons. They were always good boys.

questions.explanation: The logical order introduces Mr. Daka's pride, explains why the boys were good (they were always good boys), and then shows the long-term proof (they didn't forget their parents when grown). Option A flows naturally.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Locusts move together in a swarm. There can be over forty million locusts in a swarm. Each locust weighs about two grams. They eat anything green and growing.
options.B: Each locust weighs about two grams. Locusts move together in a swarm. There can be over forty million locusts in a swarm. They eat anything green and growing.
options.C: They eat anything green and growing. There can be over forty million locusts in a swarm. Each locust weighs about two grams. Locusts move together in a swarm.
options.D: There can be over forty million locusts in a swarm. They eat anything green and growing. Locusts move together in a swarm. Each locust weighs about two grams.

questions.explanation: The logical order introduces locusts moving in a swarm, quantifies the swarm size, describes individual locust weight, and ends with what they do (eat green things). Option A presents facts in a logical, informative sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Reporters visited Munene when he was ... years old.
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Munene had just celebrated his 114th birthday. Reporters visited him...' so he was 114 years old.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: We can conclude that yoghourt may ...
questions.explanation: Munene, who lived to 114, said 'If I had had more sense, I would eat more yoghourt!' This implies yoghourt may contribute to long life.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: We can tell from the passage that ... contributed to Munene's long life.
questions.explanation: Munene said: 'If it had not been for my third wife I would have died years ago.' This clearly states the third wife contributed to his long life.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Munene was interviewed by reporters because ...

options.A: he was the oldest in the village.

questions.explanation: The passage says reporters visited him 'to find out the secret of long life'. He was interviewed because he had lived an exceptionally long life (114 years).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage Munene was a ...
questions.explanation: Munene mentioned marrying his 'third wife', which means he had multiple wives. A person with more than one wife is called a polygamist.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Apart from the bus station, where else would you find such a timetable? At a ...
questions.explanation: A route timetable showing departure and arrival times is characteristic of transport hubs. Besides bus stations, railway stations also use timetables showing routes, departure, and arrival times.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The word departure means ...
questions.explanation: 'Departure' means the act of leaving a place. In the timetable, departure times show when the bus leaves each town.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Why is the route timetable of a bus important? It ...

options.A: guides travellers on the timing of a bus to various destinations.
options.B: provides information about bus fares to various destinations.
options.C: shows the departure time from Lusaka.
options.D: shows the arrival time in Chipata.

questions.explanation: A route timetable is important because it guides travellers on when buses depart and arrive at various destinations along the route, helping them plan their journeys.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: From the table, which route takes the longest time to travel?
questions.explanation: Calculating travel times from the timetable: Lusaka to Nyimba: 09:30 to 14:45 = 5 hours 15 minutes. Nyimba to Petauke: 14:55 to 16:10 = 1 hour 15 minutes. Sinda to Katete: 17:15 to 17:55 = 40 minutes. Katete to Chipata: 18:05 to 19:35 = 1 hour 30 minutes. Lusaka to Nyimba is the longest at 5 hours 15 minutes.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: How long does the bus take to move from Sinda to Katete?
questions.explanation: From the timetable: the bus departs Sinda at 17:15 and arrives at Katete at 17:55. The difference is 40 minutes.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Another advantage that a pencil has over a pen which is not stated in the passage is that, a pencil ...

options.B: can easily be rubbed in case of a mistake.
options.C: can be used by left handed learners.
options.D: is more expensive than a ball pen.

questions.explanation: The passage mentions: pencils are cheap, legible, light in weight, easy to carry, and never leak. 'Can be rubbed out in case of a mistake' is implied by the eraser use, but 'can be used by left handed learners' is not stated in the passage at all, making it the advantage NOT mentioned.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Cedar wood is used in the making of pencils because it is ...
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Then cedar wood is used because it is easy to sharpen.' This directly answers the question.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The writing material had a variety of names. The word variety in the passage means ...
questions.explanation: 'Variety' means a number of different types or kinds. The passage mentions 'lead, wad and plumbage' as different names (different types) for the same writing material.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage, what makes a pencil better than a ball pen? It ...

options.B: is longer than a ball pen.

questions.explanation: The passage states: 'A pencil is easy to carry around and unlike a ball pen; it never leaks.' The specific advantage over a ball pen mentioned is that it never leaks.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage, one can conclude that the process of making a pencil is ...
questions.explanation: The passage describes a multi-step process: grinding graphite, clay and water into a solution, forcing it through a tube, drying, cutting, firing in a kiln, immersing in hot oil and wax, then adding cedar wood casing. This process is clearly long and involving.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The school football ... played very well during the tournament and won a trophy.
questions.explanation: The correct collective noun for a football group of players is 'team'. A football team plays together. 'Crowd' is for spectators, 'jury' is for courts, 'group' is too generic.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Many people agree that health is ... than wealth.
questions.explanation: A comparison between two things (health and wealth) requires the comparative form. 'Better' is the irregular comparative of 'good'. 'Best' is superlative, not comparative.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The teacher ... changed the subject.
questions.explanation: An adverb is needed to modify the verb 'changed'. 'Quickly' is an adverb. 'Quick' is an adjective. 'Faster' and 'slow' do not fit the context as well.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: There was a ... of policemen at the road block.
questions.explanation: A large gathering of people at a specific location is called a 'crowd'. While 'group' is possible, the specific collective noun for a large number of people gathered is 'crowd'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Ruth walked ... along the foot bridge to avoid falling.
questions.explanation: An adverb is needed to modify the verb 'walked'. 'Carefully' is the correct adverb form. 'Careful' is an adjective. The sentence is not a comparison, so comparative/superlative forms are not needed.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: We should blame ... for failing to qualify to the World Cup.
questions.explanation: The subject is 'We', so the reflexive pronoun must be 'ourselves'. 'Themselves' is for they/them, 'myself' is for I/me, 'itself' is for it.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The engineers ... examined the bridge.
questions.explanation: The subject is 'The engineers' (third person plural), so the reflexive pronoun must be 'themselves'. 'Yourselves' is for second person plural, 'himself'/'herself' are singular.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: ... you and I must go.
questions.explanation: 'Both you and I must go' means both persons are required to go — an affirmative statement about two people. 'Both ... and' is the correct correlative conjunction here.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: ... car is parked outside there?
questions.explanation: 'Whose' is the possessive interrogative pronoun used to ask about ownership. 'Whose car is parked there?' asks who the car belongs to. 'Who's' is a contraction of 'who is'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Last year, Miyoba used to ... new shoes every week.
questions.explanation: 'Used to' is followed by the base form of the verb (infinitive without 'to'). 'Used to buy' is the correct form to express a past habit.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The woodsman was as tall as a tree.
questions.explanation: The 'as ... as' construction requires the base form of the adjective (positive degree). 'As tall as a tree' is the correct equality comparison.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The ... was very happy with comedian's performances at Mulungushi International Conference Centre yesterday.
questions.explanation: An 'audience' is the collective noun for people watching a performance. At a comedy show, the people watching are the 'audience'. 'Band' plays music, 'choir' sings, 'jury' makes judgments.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Tom's father ... a picture of Tom wearing boots and a helmet.
questions.explanation: The sentence describes a past action. 'Took' is the simple past tense of 'take'. There is no auxiliary verb, so 'taken' (past participle) cannot stand alone.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Chalwe thought ... visiting the Victoria Falls last week.
questions.explanation: The correct collocation is 'thought of' + gerund. 'Chalwe thought of visiting' means he considered the idea. 'Think of' is the standard phrasal verb.
